modules.title: Fundamentals of Sterile Compounding

courses.title: Master Formulation and Compounding Records for Sterile Compounding
General Elements of Documentation for Sterile Compounding
History of Sterile Compounding
Quality Releases and Final Checks for Sterile Compounding
Standard Operating Procedures for Sterile Compounding
USP 797 CSP Handling: Staging through Transport

mustKnow: MFR (template) vs CR (batch record) — both required; each has specific required elements that must be verified before CSP release
Quality release requires pharmacist verification of labeling, identity, quantity, integrity, and BUD before dispensing every CSP
SOPs must cover all critical operations and be reviewed at minimum every 2 years per USP 797 (2023)
2012 NECC fungal meningitis outbreak (64 deaths) → Drug Quality and Security Act 2013 → current regulatory framework

keyConcepts.definition: Master template specifying formula, ingredients, equipment, stability data, storage, and BUD basis — never modified per batch
Lot-specific batch record documenting actual ingredients used, calculations, personnel, QA checks, and pharmacist sign-off
Every deviation, yield calculation, labeling step, and final check must be traceable and retrievable for any CSP lot
Written step-by-step instructions for all critical processes — must be immediately accessible at point of use, dated, versioned, and signed
Maintain temperature-controlled chain, tamper-evident packaging, complete patient labeling from preparation through delivery

modules.clinicalConnection: The MFR/CR chain is your audit trail when something goes wrong. During any adverse event investigation or accreditation survey, reviewers will trace every IV bag back to its batch record. Incomplete or missing documentation is a direct patient safety risk and a regulatory finding. Make this your non-negotiable standard from day one.

modules.title: Engineering Controls for Sterile Compounding

courses.title: USP 797 Primary Engineering Controls (PEC)
USP 797 Secondary Engineering Controls (SEC)

mustKnow: PEC type determines HD suitability — positive-pressure LAF is NEVER appropriate for hazardous drug compounding; must use BSC or CACI
SEC design determines your max achievable BUD: SCA = Category 1 only (12h/24h), ISO 7 cleanroom suite = Category 2 eligible (45+ days)
ISO 7 buffer room: ≥30 ACPH total with ≥15 ACPH from HEPA; maintained at positive pressure relative to anteroom
PEC and SEC must be certified every 6 months by a qualified Certifying Organization (CO) — airflow velocity, HEPA integrity, particle counts

keyConcepts.definition: LAF workbench (positive pressure, non-HD) · BSC Class II (negative pressure, for HD) · CACI/isolator (closed-system, highest protection)
ISO 7 buffer room + ISO 8 anteroom = full cleanroom suite (Category 2 eligible) vs SCA = ISO 5 PEC in non-classified room (Category 1 only)
Regardless of hood quality, an ISO 5 PEC in a non-classified space can only produce Category 1 CSPs — 12h CRT, 24h refrigerated maximum BUD
Positive pressure buffer room pushes contaminants outward; C-SEC negative pressure contains HD vapors and aerosolized particles inward
HEPA-filtered pass-through between anteroom and buffer room required for material transfer without compromising pressure differential

modules.clinicalConnection: Your engineering control configuration directly dictates what BUDs you can assign. Operating from an SCA caps you at 12-hour room-temperature BUDs — a fundamental operational constraint for outpatient infusion. Understanding this link between physical space and BUD policy is critical for pharmacy leadership decisions about facility design and program scope.

modules.title: Personnel Sampling Metrics

courses.title: Personnel Competency Testing in Aseptic Manipulation

mustKnow: Initial qualification requires 3 separate satisfactory passes for both GFS and media fill before any independent compounding is permitted
GFS action level: ≤1 CFU per gloved hand at ISO 5 — any growth above this triggers investigation, retraining, and re-qualification
Media fill failure = immediate suspension from independent compounding; 3 consecutive passing re-qualifications required to return
Ongoing competency: every 6 months for all compounders — both GFS and media fill; triggered earlier by any technique failure or EM excursion

keyConcepts.definition: Both hands, all 5 fingertips + thumbs imprinted on TSA contact plates immediately after final garbing step — tests garbing quality and hand hygiene technique
Fills simulated doses using soybean-casein digest (SCD) broth under actual compounding conditions; incubated 14 days at two temperature ranges (20–25°C and 30–35°C)
3 passes required to establish initial qualification; then every 6 months regardless of role — any growth = failure, any gap in documentation = unqualified
Trained assessor must observe full hand hygiene and garbing sequence and document compliance against written SOP — cannot self-assess
All qualification records (dates, results, pass/fail, remediation, sign-offs) must be maintained, organized, and immediately retrievable on request

modules.clinicalConnection: As the supervising pharmacist, you are accountable for every compounder's qualification status. Before allowing a technician to compound independently, verify documented initial qualification exists (3 passes, all signed). Accreditation surveyors will ask for these records by individual name. A gap in any record = immediate finding. Build a living tracker and audit it quarterly.

modules.title: Viable Facility Sampling Metrics

courses.title: Overview of Environmental Monitoring
Investigation and Remediation of Viable EM and Personnel Sampling Excursions

mustKnow: Action level excursion = immediate cessation of compounding, root cause investigation, CAPA, documentation, and re-sampling — in that order
Alert level = signal requiring investigation and increased monitoring frequency — does not automatically halt operations
USP 797 (2023) viable EM sampling frequency: monthly for ISO 5 and ISO 7; quarterly minimum for ISO 8 anteroom
Common EM excursion sources: garbing failures, poor aseptic technique, HVAC issues, inadequate cleaning, excessive traffic in classified areas

keyConcepts.definition: Settle plates, contact/RODAC plates, and active air samplers — measure colony-forming units (CFU) to detect microbial contamination
Electronic particle counters measuring airborne particle concentration (0.5 µm, 5.0 µm) — separate program from viable EM, both required
Defined locations for each ISO zone — ISO 5 work surface, ISO 7 floor/walls/supply vents, ISO 8 anteroom — must be pre-specified in written EM program
Corrective Action (address the immediate failure) + Preventive Action (prevent recurrence) — time-bound, documented, with re-sampling for verification
Upward trending CFU counts warrant investigation even before hitting formal action levels — the EM program should be reviewed monthly for trends

modules.clinicalConnection: When an action-level excursion occurs, you must decide whether to halt compounding — a decision with direct patient supply consequences. Having a pre-written, pharmacist-approved excursion response protocol means this decision is made calmly in advance, not reactively during a crisis. Write it before you ever need it.

modules.title: Sanitization of Pharmacy Controlled Environments

courses.title: Sterile Compounding Environments: Principles of Cleaning and Disinfection
Cleaning of Primary Engineering Controls for Sterile Compounding
Cleaning of Secondary Engineering Controls for Sterile Compounding

mustKnow: Cleaning must always precede disinfection — organic debris neutralizes disinfectant activity; skipping or reversing this order is a critical compliance failure
Sterile 70% IPA (not standard unsterilized IPA) is required inside ISO 5 and ISO 7 classified areas — the word 'sterile' is load-bearing
Sporicidal agent required at minimum monthly for all ISO-classified areas per USP 797 (2023); must be documented and rotated to prevent resistance
PEC cleaning: at beginning of each production shift, after any potential contamination event, after every ~30 min of continuous use, and at end of shift

keyConcepts.definition: Cleaning (physical debris removal, first step always) → disinfection (microbial reduction) → sterilization (complete kill) — each step is distinct and sequential
Always proceed from cleanest to most contaminated and top-to-bottom: anteroom → buffer room → PEC interior; never reverse this flow
Sterile 70% IPA for routine; sporicidal agents (sodium hypochlorite 1:10, peracetic acid, VHP) for scheduled sporicidal sessions; document agent lot, date, and contact times
The most commonly missed step — disinfectant must remain wet on surface for the full required contact time (varies by agent: 30 seconds to 10 minutes)
Wipe side panels → back panel → work surface using overlapping strokes; sterile low-lint wipes; one direction only; do not wipe back into already-cleaned areas

modules.clinicalConnection: Cleaning SOPs must be specific: which agent, which surface, dwell time required, wipe technique, and documentation requirements. Inspectors compare actual observed practices to your written SOPs in real time. The two most common surveyor findings: non-sterile IPA used inside classified areas, and no documented sporicidal agent rotation schedule.

modules.title: Aseptic Technique and Related Work Practices

courses.title: Aseptic Technique and Conduct for Sterile Compounding
Use of Supplies and Components
Best Practices for USP 797 Material Handling
Best Practices for Mixing Outside of ISO-Classified Conditions (USP 797)
Quality Management of Sterile Compounding

mustKnow: Critical site = any opening into the sterile system (needle tip, vial septum, bag port, syringe tip) — must never be shadowed or touched at any point
Garbing order: shoe covers → hair/beard cover → face mask → hand hygiene (30-sec scrub to elbows) → gown → sterile gloves (last, after gown)
All supply items wiped with sterile 70% IPA at the pass-through before entering the buffer room — this step is never optional, never abbreviated
Immediate-use: single patient, single dose, no preservatives, administration starts ≤1 hour from prep start — not a convenience tool; strict criteria

keyConcepts.definition: Every critical site must remain continuously in the unidirectional HEPA airstream (first air) from ISO 5 — this is the foundational principle of aseptic technique
Any action that blocks first air to a critical site (crossing hand over open vial, placing a syringe body between hood airflow and needle tip) — most common media fill failure
Maintain plunger grip without contaminating plunger, avoid vial coring, single-hand needle recap only, never lay an uncapped syringe flat on the hood surface
All supply items wiped with sterile IPA at pass-through window before entering buffer room; pre-staged in anteroom, not inside the PEC itself
Sterility assurance level (SAL) targets — Category 1 SAL 10⁻³, Category 2 SAL 10⁻⁶; includes error reporting, near-miss capture, and corrective action loops

modules.clinicalConnection: As the final quality gate before dispensing, you must recognize aseptic technique breaks during technician observation in real time. Shadowing is subtle and often unconscious — technicians frequently shadow without awareness. Train your eye to watch the critical sites continuously, not the compounding action. That shift in focus is the difference between catching a break and missing one.

modules.title: Nonsterile to Sterile Compounding Practices

courses.title: Steam and Dry-Heat Sterilization Methods
Sterilization by Filtration
Bacterial Endotoxin (Pyrogen) Testing
Sterility Testing Requirements of USP <71> and 797

mustKnow: 0.22 µm filtration removes bacteria but NOT endotoxins — filtration sterilization and bacterial endotoxin testing (BET) are two separate, independent requirements
Autoclaving (121°C, 15 psi, ≥15 min) sterilizes but does NOT depyrogenate — dry heat at 250°C required to destroy endotoxins from glassware and equipment
Sterility testing (USP <71>) extends BUD for Category 2 CSPs only — no effect on Category 1; this is the pathway from 45-day to 90-day room temperature BUD
Intrathecal route carries the most stringent endotoxin limit (0.2 EU/kg/hr) — errors here cause fever, seizures, and death; no tolerance for BET failure

keyConcepts.definition: Moist heat kills bacteria, fungi, spores, viruses — 121°C at 15 psi for ≥15 min minimum; gold standard for aqueous solutions and heat-stable equipment
160–180°C for 1–2 hours for oils/anhydrous vehicles/glassware; 250°C for depyrogenation — kills AND removes endotoxins from equipment surfaces
0.22 µm (or 0.2 µm) membrane filter removes bacteria and fungi from heat-labile drug solutions — does not remove viruses, prions, or endotoxins
Limulus Amebocyte Lysate detects gram-negative bacterial endotoxins; required for all aqueous parenterals; intrathecal limit = 0.2 EU/kg/hr (most stringent)
14-day incubation at two temperature ranges (20–25°C and 30–35°C); direct inoculation or membrane filtration method; any growth = failure, batch rejection

modules.clinicalConnection: For OPAT or specialty programs that batch-compound at scale (vancomycin, cefepime, etc.), sterility testing is the operational lever that extends BUD from 45 to 90 days at room temperature. That difference nearly doubles your shelf life, directly impacting batch planning, waste rates, and patient care continuity. Understanding when and how to implement sterility testing is a high-value pharmacist competency.

modules.title: Requirements and Best Practices — Hazardous Drug Compounding

courses.title: Hazardous Drug Overview (USP 795 and 797)
Containment Primary and Secondary Engineering Controls (C-PEC and C-SEC)
Personal Protective Equipment for HD Handling (USP 795 and 797)
HD Work Practice: Receiving through Transport to Patients
HD Work Practice: Decontamination and Spill Management (USP 795 and 797)

mustKnow: C-PEC must be negative pressure — a standard positive-pressure LAF workbench is categorically never acceptable for HD compounding under any circumstances
Deactivation precedes decontamination — deactivation chemically neutralizes the HD; decontamination then physically removes residue; reversing the order defeats both steps
NIOSH HD list: Group 1 = antineoplastics (highest risk), Group 2 = non-antineoplastics requiring special handling, Group 3 = reproductive hazard only
USP 800 applies to ALL personnel handling HDs — receiving staff, nursing staff hanging chemo, transport staff — not pharmacy only; documented training required for everyone

keyConcepts.definition: Updated annually — verify your facility's HD list against the current NIOSH publication; drugs are added and reclassified; your policy must reflect the current list
BSC Class II Type B2 (100% HEPA-exhausted to outside) or negative-pressure closed-system isolator — must maintain negative pressure relative to the room at all times
Negative pressure room containing the C-PEC; pressure differential -0.01 to -0.03 inches WC relative to adjacent areas; HEPA-filtered or dedicated exhaust that does not recirculate air
Double chemotherapy-rated gloves (ASTM D6978 tested) + chemo gown (AAMI PB70 Level 2 minimum) + eye/face protection for splash risk + N95/P100 respirator for volatile HDs
Deactivate with sodium hypochlorite solution (most antineoplastics), then decontaminate with detergent and water, then disinfect with approved agent — three distinct sequential steps

modules.clinicalConnection: USP 800 extends your accountability beyond the cleanroom. Nursing staff who hang chemotherapy bags, receiving clerks who handle HD deliveries, and any pharmacy staff who touch HD containers all require documented USP 800 training and access to appropriate PPE. Occupational HD exposure is a long-latency health risk — and as the pharmacist, you are the subject-matter expert your institution depends on to get this right.
